#Text=vegetables
#Text=
#Text=
#Text= it is practical to can all vegetables, even such difficult ones as
#Text= corn, peas and beans, by the cold-pack method of canning without using
#Text= any preservatives, if you will follow all directions, instructions and
#Text= the time-table accurately. vegetable canning is a little more
#Text= complicated than fruit canning.
#Text=tomatoes
#Text=
#Text= every one likes canned tomatoes. in many homes more tomatoes are
#Text= canned than any other product. the housewife uses them for soups, for
#Text= sauces and for seasoning many meat dishes. some women say: "i can
#Text= preserve everything but tomatoes. they always spoil. what do i do
#Text= wrong?"
#Text=if the following directions are followed tomatoes will not
#Text= spoil.
#Text=tomatoes really are the easiest vegetable to can, because the period
#Text= of sterilization is short, and many jars may be canned in a day, or if
#Text= one is very busy a few jars may be canned daily without the
#Text=expenditure of a great deal of time.
#Text=the best tomatoes for canning are those of moderate size, smooth and
#Text= uniformly ripe. when a tomato ripens unevenly or when it is misshapen,
#Text= it is difficult to peel, and the percentage of waste is high. tomatoes
#Text= should not be picked when they are green or partly ripe, for the
#Text= flavor will not be so good as when they are allowed to remain upon
#Text= the vines until fully ripe. care should be taken, however, not to
#Text= allow them to become overripe before canning.
#Text=in no instance should a tomato with a rotten spot be canned, even
#Text= though the spot is cut out, for the occasional spoiled jar resulting
#Text= from this attempted saving will cost more than the partly spoiled
#Text= tomatoes are worth. if the housewife will can only uniformly ripe,
#Text= sound tomatoes, saving the small, uneven but sound fruit for tomato
#Text= _purée_, she will have a much better-looking pack and greater food
#Text= value at the close of the season. yellow tomatoes may be canned in the
#Text= same manner as are the more common red varieties, except that it is
#Text= not necessary to remove the cores.
#Text=first of all, grade for ripeness, size and quality; this is to insure
#Text= a high-grade product. we could, of course, can different sizes and
#Text= shades together, but uniform products are more pleasing to the eye and
#Text= will sterilize much more evenly. if the products are of the same
#Text= ripeness and quality, the entire pack will receive the proper degree
#Text= of cooking.
#Text=wash the tomatoes. have ready a kettle of boiling water. put the
#Text= tomatoes in a wire basket, or lay them on a piece of cheesecloth or a
#Text= towel, twist the ends together to form a sack, and let this down into
#Text= the kettle. it is a good plan to slip a rubber band round the neck of
#Text= this sack to hold the ends in place. the ends should be long enough to
#Text= stand up out of the water and so avoid danger of burning the fingers
#Text= when removing the product.
#Text=have the water boiling hard. lower the tomatoes into the boiling
#Text= water. this is called scalding the tomatoes. we scald the tomatoes to
#Text= loosen the skin. if the tomatoes are very ripe, one minute scalding
#Text= will be sufficient. the average length of time for tomatoes, just
#Text= perfect for canning, is one and a half minutes. do not leave the
#Text=place each jar in the canner as it is packed. the first jars in will
#Text= not be affected by the extra cooking. have the water just below the
#Text= boiling point as you put in each jar. when you have the canner full
#Text= bring the water to the boiling point as quickly as possible and begin
#Text= to count cooking or sterilizing time from the moment it does boil.
#Text=some women make the mistake at the end of the cooking period of
#Text= letting the jars remain in the boiling water, standing on the false
#Text= bottom of the canner until they are cool enough to handle with no
#Text= danger of burning the hands. this slow method of cooling not only
#Text= tends to create "flat sour," but it is apt to result in cloudy-looking
#Text= jars and in mushy vegetables.
#Text=for this reason you should have in your equipment a lifter with which
#Text= you can lift out the hot jars without the hands touching them. if you
#Text= use a rack with wire handles this answers the same purpose.
#Text=this "flat sour," which is not at all dangerous from the standpoint of
#Text= health, must not be confused with the botulinus bacteria, which is an
#Text= entirely different thing.
#Text="flat sour," perfectly harmless, appears often with inexperienced
#Text= canners. botulinus, harmful, appears rarely. you need not be at all
#Text= alarmed about eating either "flat sour" or botulinus, because the odor
#Text= from spoiled goods is so distasteful--it really resembles rancid
#Text= cheese--that you would never get a spoon of it to your mouth.
#Text=if you are debating whether this jar or that jar of soup or vegetables
#Text= is spoiled, do not _taste_ the contents of the jar.
#Text=_smell_ it.
#Text=tasting might poison you if you happened on the botulinus bacteria,
#Text= which is so rare it need alarm no one; whereas smelling is perfectly
#Text= safe.

#Text=by employing this test, sugar can often be reduced, and thus the jelly

#Text=after the fruit has been selected and prepared as usual by washing,
#Text= stemming, and so forth, it is ready to be heated in an acid-proof
#Text= kettle. with juicy fruits use just enough water to prevent
#Text= burning--about one cup of water to every four or five quarts of fruit.
#Text=the juicy fruits are currants, raspberries, and so forth. with less
#Text= juicy fruits, as apples or quinces, use enough water to cover, or
#Text= follow the rule, half as much water as fruit. use the cores, skins and
#Text= seeds; these improve the flavor and color of the jelly.
#Text=berries can be mashed. heat the fruit slowly in a covered kettle,
#Text= stirring once in a while to obtain an even cooking. when the simmering
#Text= point is reached, crush the fruit with a well-soaked wooden masher.
#Text=when the fruit is tender or has a transparent appearance, it is ready
#Text= to strain.
#Text=and then boil it. after it is boiled, remove all meat and make a mock
#Text= turtle stew or ragout. prepare the tripe as for table use and then
#Text= can.
#Text=after the soup stock is made and the bones are cracked for a second
#Text= cooking, the bones need not be thrown away. you can dry them, run them
#Text= through a bone crusher and either feed them to the chickens or use
#Text= them for fertilizer. in this way not a particle of the dressed animal
#Text= is wasted.
#Text=here are a few ways to utilize the cuts that are really "left-overs."
#Text=goulash
#Text=
#Text= 2 pounds of meat scraps which can consist of beef, veal or pork.
#Text= 2 ounces of any fat.
#Text= 2 onions chopped fine.
#Text= 1 stalk celery, cut in small pieces.
#Text= 2 carrots.
#Text=2 cups tomatoes either canned or fresh.
#Text= 1 bay leaf.
#Text= 6 whole cloves.
#Text= 6 peppercorns.
#Text= 1 blade mace or a little thyme or both.
#Text=a little flour.
#Text= 1 tablespoonful chopped parsley.
#Text=salt and paprika to taste.
#Text=cut the meat into one inch squares and roll in flour. melt the fat in
#Text= the frying pan, add the vegetables (onions, celery, carrots) and brown
#Text= lightly: add the meat and brown. stir with a spoon or fork to prevent
#Text= burning. when browned empty into a pan.
#Text=put the bay leaf, cloves, peppercorns, mace and thyme into a
#Text= cheesecloth bag and add to the meat, add tomatoes. cover with soup
#Text= stock or water and simmer 45 minutes if it is going to be canned. if
#Text= for immediate use, 2 hours will be necessary to thoroughly cook it.
#Text=remove the spices, season with salt, paprika and the chopped parsley.
#Text=you can add worcestershire sauce or soy sauce if desired. use only
#Text= small quantities as these sauces are very strong in their distinctive
#Text= flavor. put hot mixture into cans and sterilize.
#Text=if the different spices are not at hand a good goulash can be made by
#Text= using the meat, fat, onions, tomatoes, flour, salt and pepper and
#Text= omitting the rest of the recipe.
#Text=liver sausage
#Text=
#Text= beef, veal, or hog liver. remove the membrane and cut away the large
#Text= blood vessels. soak in water 1 to 2 hours to draw out blood. boil
#Text= until done. when cooled put through a food chopper or grate finely.
#Text=take half as much boiled fat pork as liver. divide this fat into two
#Text= portions; chop one portion into one-quarter inch cubes; pass the other
#Text= portion through the food chopper; mix all together thoroughly; add
#Text= salt, ground cloves, pepper, and a little grated onion to taste. a
#Text= little thyme and marjoram may be added to suit taste.
#Text=(for a liver
#Text= weighing 1½ pounds add ¾ pounds fat pork, 3 to 4 teaspoonfuls salt, ½
#Text=from your own tin-canned products than from tin-canned food bought at
#Text= the store. most canned foods if in a spoiled condition readily show
#Text= this condition by the swelling of the can or by odor or taste. canned
#Text= foods showing such evidences of decomposition should not be used.
#Text=certain foods which are high in protein, such as meats, peas, beans
#Text= and fish products, may undergo decomposition without making this
#Text= condition obvious to the senses. it is essential, therefore, that the
#Text= greatest care be taken to subject such products to proper preparation
#Text= and ample processing. it should be remembered that canned foods, after
#Text= opening the containers, should be treated as perishable products and
#Text= should be handled with the same precautions that are applied when
#Text= fresh products are being used.
#Text=advantages of cans
#Text=
#Text= many housewives ask, "why can in tin when we have always used glass
#Text= jars?"
#Text=there are many advantages in canning in tin which we can well
#Text= consider. there is no breakage as in glass; you can handle the tin
#Text=cans as carelessly as you choose and you will not hear a snap or crack
#Text= indicating a lost jar. furthermore, tin cans are easier to handle not
#Text= only in canning but in storing.
#Text=the expense each year of new tin covers or new tin cans is no more
#Text= than the purchase of new rubbers and the replacement of broken glass
#Text= jars. furthermore, one big advantage of tin over glass is that tin
#Text= cans can be cooled quickly by plunging them into cold water
#Text= immediately upon removal from the canner, and thus the cooking is
#Text= stopped at the proper moment. the product is consequently better in
#Text= form and flavor than when the cooking is prolonged, as it must be in
#Text= glass jars. many women like the large openings of cans because they
#Text= can make better packs than when using narrow-necked jars.
#Text=if you do not care to bother with the soldering you can purchase a
#Text= safe and simple device that will do the work for you. this device is
#Text= called a tin-can sealer. with a sealer no soldering is necessary.
#Text=even an inexperienced person, by following directions carefully, can
#Text= seal a can as well as an experienced one. the sealed cans look exactly
#Text= like those purchased at the store. two or three cans a minute can be
#Text=
#Text= flat-sour often causes annoyance to beginners in canning some
#Text= vegetables, such as corn, peas, beans and asparagus. these canned
#Text= foods may show no signs of spoilage and yet when the can is opened the
#Text= product may have a sour taste and a disagreeable odor. this
#Text= "flat-sour" is not harmful and must not be confused with "botulinus,"
#Text= which is harmful. however, the taste and odor are so disagreeable you
#Text= will have no desire to eat "flat-sour" canned goods.
#Text=this trouble can be avoided if you will use fresh products, that is,
#Text= those which have not been allowed to wilt or stand around the shops
#Text= for several days, and will blanch, cold-dip, and pack one jar of
#Text= product at a time, and place each jar in the canner as it is packed.
#Text=the first jars in will not be affected by the extra cooking. when the
#Text= steam-pressure canner is used the jars or cans may be placed in the
#Text= retort and the cover placed into position but not clamped down until
#Text= the retort is filled.

#Text=these two objections seem to be expressed more than any other. and in
#Text= addition there is a third objection to drying: i want my prepared

column1: #Text= food ready to use on a minute  '  s notice. i can quickly open a can of my
#Text= fruit and vegetables and there it is ready. with my dried things i
#Text= have to allow time for soaking and cooking." this we will have to
#Text= admit is true. but what weight have these three arguments against the
#Text= many advantages of drying?
#Text=when we study the history of food preservation we find that drying was
#Text= practiced before canning, pickling or preserving. i know my
#Text= grandmother successfully dried quantities of things.
#Text=vegetable and fruit drying have been little practiced for a
#Text= generation or more, though there have been some thrifty housekeepers
#Text= who have clung to their dried corn, peas, beans and apples. a friend
#Text=of mine says: "why, dried corn has a much better, sweeter taste than
#Text= your canned stuff. i would rather have one little dish of my delicious
#Text= dried corn than two big dishes of your canned corn."
#Text=drying, i think we will all admit, does not and cannot take the place
#Text= of canning fruits and vegetables in glass or tin. drying and canning
#Text= are twin sisters, and always go hand in hand.
#Text=the ideal arrangement for all homes, whether on the farm, in the
#Text= village, in the town or in the city, is to have an ample supply of
#Text= canned food for emergencies and quick service, and an equally ample
#Text= supply of dried foods when meals are planned beforehand and there is
#Text= time enough for the soaking and cooking of the dried foods.
#Text=the advantages of drying
#Text=
#Text= when we come right down to facts, drying has many advantages over
#Text= canning.
#Text=the process is very simple, as you will see. the cost is slight. in
#Text= almost every home the necessary equipment, in its simplest form, is
#Text= already at hand. there is no expense for glass jars or tin cans, and
#Text= with ordinary care there is no loss of products, as there may be in
#Text= handling glass jars or from spoilage. the actual work requires less
#Text= time and less skill than canning and the dried products when properly
#Text= prepared are just as good as the canned ones--some say better.
#Text=one special thing in favor of drying is the little storage space
#Text= needed. you can often reduce 100 pounds of fresh product to ten
#Text= pounds by drying, without any loss of food value and with little loss
#Text= of flavor.
#Text=dried products can be moved more conveniently than glass jars or tin
#Text= cans, for they are usually reduced to from one-third to one-fifth of
#Text= the original bulk.
#Text=another valuable thing about drying is that the little odds and ends
#Text= one would scarcely bother to can may be dried in the oven as you go
#Text= about your housework.
#Text=i have often been asked the difference between the meaning of the
#Text= terms "evaporated," "dried," "desiccated" and "dehydrated." these
#Text= terms are used more or less interchangeably when applied to foods from
#Text= which the moisture has been removed. in a general way, however,
#Text= "evaporated" products are those from which the moisture has been
#Text= removed through the agency of artificial heat; dried fruit is that
#Text= which has been exposed to the heat of the sun, though not infrequently
#Text= the term is applied to products handled in the evaporator. the other
#Text= terms are commonly applied to products that have been evaporated by
#Text= one of the various patented processes in which equipment of some
#Text= special design has been used.
#Text=to avoid any confusion we will use the general term "dried" for all
#Text= products that have enough of the water removed to prevent bacterial
#Text= action, but which still retain the maximum food value, color and
#Text= flavor of the original product. and that is what we want to accomplish
#Text= when we attempt to dry.
#Text=how are we to remove the water and still retain food value, color and
#Text=flavor?
#Text=there are three principal methods by which we can do this.
#Text=first, by artificial heat. drying by artificial heat is done in the
#Text= oven or on top of a cookstove or range, in trays suspended on the
#Text= stove or in a specially constructed dryer built at home or purchased.
#Text=second, by the sun. sun drying is done either out of doors in the sun,
#Text= under glass in sun parlors, or the products are hung in the attic
#Text= where the sun has free access.
#Text=third, satisfactory drying may be done by an air blast from an
#Text= electric fan.
#Text=of course any one of these may be used alone or two different methods
#Text= may be combined. you can start a product on the stove and finish it in
#Text= the sun, or _vice versa._
#Text=
#Text= the simplest and yet the most effective drying may be done on plates
#Text= or dishes placed in the oven. it may be done on the back of the
#Text= kitchen stove with these same utensils while the oven is being used
#Text= for baking. in this way left-overs and other bits of food may be dried
#Text=
#Text= green tomatoes. green tomatoes should be packed whole and prepared
#Text= as cucumbers. the dill and spice may be added if desired.
#Text=beets. beets must be scrubbed thoroughly and packed whole. if peeled
#Text= or sliced before being fermented the beets lose considerable color and
#Text= flavor.
#Text=beet tops and turnip tops. these should be washed thoroughly and
#Text= packed into the container without being cut up.
#Text=peas. green peas should be shelled and packed in the same way as
#Text= string beans. it is advisable to use fairly small containers for peas,
#Text= so that the quantity opened up will be used before it has a chance to
#Text= spoil.
#Text=corn. husk and clean the silk from the corn; wash and place the ears
#Text= on end in the jar, packing the jar nearly full. pour the brine over
#Text= the ears; add cover and weights. fermented corn has a sour taste,
#Text= which may not be relished if the corn is eaten alone. for this reason
#Text=it will be preferable in most cases to preserve corn by canning,
#Text= drying or by salting without fermentation. fermented corn, however,
#Text= may be used in the preparation of some dishes, such as chowders,
#Text= omelets, and so forth, where its flavor will be masked to some extent
#Text= by the other ingredients. to some people this peculiar acid taste of
#Text= fermented corn is not at all objectionable.
#Text=salting without fermentation. in this method the vegetables are
#Text= packed with enough salt to prevent fermentation or the growth of
#Text= yeasts or molds. the vegetables preserved most satisfactorily by this
#Text= method are dandelions, beet tops, turnip tops, spinach, kale, chard,
#Text= cabbage, cauliflower, string beans, green peas and corn. the following
#Text= directions should be followed:
#Text=
#Text= the vegetables should be washed, drained and weighed. the amount of
#Text= salt needed will be a quarter of the weight of the vegetables. kegs or
#Text= crocks make satisfactory containers. put a layer of vegetables about
#Text= an inch thick on the bottom of the container. cover this with salt.
#Text=continue making alternate layers of vegetables and salt until the
#Text= container is almost filled. the salt should be evenly distributed so
